insight: There's been no measurable rain lately, so there's no runoff driving contamination, and strong summer sunlight is helping disinfect the water. The model's main signals right now: hours since high tide is lowering risk — the timing relative to high tide affects how concentrated bacteria are at the shoreline; the lack of recent rain is keeping runoff-driven contamination low; and wave period is lowering risk — longer-period swell reaches deeper and can resuspend sediment-bound bacteria. The last lab sample (10 MPN/100mL) is now 28 days old — beaches are sampled about weekly, so a reading this stale carries little weight, and this estimate leans on current conditions.

There's no recent lab sample to anchor this (the last was 31 days ago), so the estimate is based entirely on current environmental conditions. It's a naturally clean, well-flushed open-coast beach (unsafe only ~3% of the time), so outside of storms it's reliably safe.
